assumption: LLM agent takes 3.0s to think per move (typical single-call latency for a frontier or mid-tier model with a few hundred tokens of reasoning + tool call)
method: overhead_fraction = env_overhead / (thinking_time + env_overhead); env_overhead varies by scenario per environment

scenarios.note: irrelevant in practice: no LLM would use CartPole as an agent benchmark; included as an in-process reference point
turn-based multi-agent in-process env; no network hop, no tick

scenarios.env_overhead_used: measured p50 order/state request, 21.0ms (from bench/findings.md, 2026-07-06 live match)

scenarios.environment: AndroidWars REST + tick-alignment wait, worst case
scenarios.env_overhead_used: 21ms request + full 2.0s tick wait (agent action submitted just after a tick boundary, must wait a full period before its effect is visible)
scenarios.caveat: this is the pessimistic bound; it is not request overhead, it is game-loop cadence shared by any tick-based simulation (real-time strategy games are not request-driven)

scenarios.environment: AndroidWars REST + tick-alignment wait, average case
scenarios.env_overhead_used: 21ms request + mean 1.0s tick wait (uniform arrival over the 2.0s tick window)
reported turn deadlines range 5 minutes to 10 days; even the fastest live games use 5-30 minute phases
scenarios.overhead_fraction_pct: not comparable — deadline is a fairness/coordination window shared by all 7 players, not per-request overhead; a 3s-thinking agent uses <0.01% of even a 5-minute deadline

takeaway: Environment overhead is never the bottleneck for an LLM agent that spends seconds thinking per move. In-process gym-style envs add a few microseconds (negligible, matters only for RL training loops with millions of steps and no 'thinking' cost). AndroidWars' 21ms REST call is also negligible (0.7% of wall time). The only overhead worth naming is the 2.0s tick cadence itself, which behaves like a shared coordination window (same category as Diplomacy's turn deadlines, just far shorter) rather than a per-call cost -- and even in the worst-case alignment it adds at most 2s to a move, comparable to or smaller than the agent's own thinking time.
